title: Mixtures and combined rates word problems
description: Determine individual rates and overall outcomes from real world contexts involving combinations of different speeds or concentrations.
topic_path: Mixtures and combined rates word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
Ujarak has $100\,\text{mL}$ of a strong toothpaste with a $1.1\%$ concentration of sodium fluoride. They have a weaker toothpaste with a $0.3\%$ concentration of sodium fluoride.

**What volume, in milliliters, of the weaker toothpaste would Ujarak need to add to the strong toothpaste to create a blend with a $0.8\%$ concentration of sodium fluoride?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{mL}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}}{\redE{\text{total volume}}}$

Since the total volume of the toothpaste will be greater than $0\,\text{mL}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $v$ to represent the volume, in milliliters, of the weaker toothpaste and solve for $v$.

###Representing the total volume

There are $100\,\text{mL}$ of the strong toothpaste and $v\,\text{mL}$ of the weaker toothpaste.

The total volume is $\redE{(100+v)\,\text{mL}}$.

###Representing the ingredient volume

The same relationship among concentration, total volume, and ingredient volume exists for each toothpaste.

The strong toothpaste has a $\goldE{1.1\%}$ concentration of sodium fluoride.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.011} \cdot \redE{100\,\text{mL}} &= \blueE{1.1\,\text{mL}}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{1.1\,\text{mL}}$ of sodium fluoride in the strong toothpaste.

The weaker toothpaste has a $\goldE{0.3\%}$ concentration of sodium fluoride.

$\goldE{0.003} \cdot \redE{v\,\text{mL}} = \blueE{0.003v\,\text{mL}}$

There are $\blueE{0.003v\,\text{mL}}$ of sodium fluoride in the weaker toothpaste.

There will be $\blueE{(1.1 + 0.003v) \,\text{mL}}$ of sodium fluoride in the mixture.

###Putting it together and solving

The mixed toothpaste needs to have a $\goldE{0.8\%}$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.008}\cdot \redE{(100+v)} &= \blueE{1.1 + 0.003v}\\\\
0.8+0.008v &= 1.1 + 0.003v\\\\
0.005v &= 0.3\\\\
v&=60
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

Ujarak needs to mix $60\,\text{mL}$ of the weaker toothpaste with the stronger toothpaste.

assessment_items.question_content:
Katlego mixed a paint hue that is $40\%$ red and $60\%$ yellow. She has $3\,\text{mL}$ of the mix.

**What volume, in milliliters, of pure yellow paint should Katlego add to have a hue that is $70\%$ yellow?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{mL}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}}{\redE{\text{total volume}}}$

Since the total volume of the paint will be greater than $0\,\text{mL}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $v$ to represent the volume, in milliliters, of the pure yellow and solve for $v$.

###Representing the total volume

There are $3\,\text{mL}$ of the original paint mixture and $v\,\text{mL}$ of the pure yellow.

The total volume is $\redE{(3+v)\,\text{mL}}$.

###Representing the ingredient volume

The same relationship among concentration, total volume, and ingredient volume exists for each paint.

The original mixture has a $\goldE{60\%}$ concentration of yellow.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.6} \cdot \redE{3\,\text{mL}} &= \blueE{1.8\,\text{mL}}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{1.8\,\text{mL}}$ of yellow paint in the original mixture.

The pure yellow paint has a $\goldE{100\%}$ concentration of yellow.

$\goldE{1} \cdot \redE{v\,\text{mL}} = \blueE{v\,\text{mL}}$

There are $\blueE{v\,\text{mL}}$ of yellow paint in the pure yellow paint.

There will be $\blueE{(1.8 + v) \,\text{mL}}$ of yellow paint in the new mixture.

###Putting it together and solving

The new mixture needs to have a $70\%$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.7}\cdot \redE{(3+v)} &= \blueE{1.8 + v}\\\\
2.1+0.7v &= 1.8 + v\\\\
0.3 &= 0.3v\\\\
1 &=v
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

Katlego should add $1\,\text{mL}$ of pure yellow paint to the mixture.

assessment_items.question_content:
There is a $30\%$ concentration of cobalt chloride in $90\,\text{g}$ of a water and cobalt chloride [[☃ definition 1]].

**What mass, in grams, of water has to evaporate to have a $40\%$ concentration of cobalt chloride?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{g}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}}{\redE{\text{total mass}}}$

Since the total mass of the solution will be greater than $0\,\text{g}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $m$ to represent the mass, in grams, of the evaporated water and solve for $m$.

###Representing the total mass

There are $90\,\text{g}$ of the original solution and $m\,\text{g}$ of evaporated water.

The total mass is $\redE{(90-m)\,\text{g}}$.

###Representing the ingredient mass

The same relationship among concentration, total mass, and ingredient mass exists for each solution.

The original solution has a $\goldE{30\%}$ concentration of cobalt chloride.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}\\\\
\goldE{0.3} \cdot \redE{90\,\text{g}} &= \blueE{27\,\text{g}}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{27\,\text{g}}$ of cobalt chloride in the original solution.

The water that evaporates has a $\goldE{0\%}$ concentration of cobalt chloride.

$\goldE{0} \cdot \redE{m\,\text{g}} = \blueE{0\,\text{g}}$

There are $\blueE{0\,\text{g}}$ of cobalt chloride in the water that evaporates.

There will be $\blueE{(27-0) \,\text{g}}$ of cobalt chloride in the new solution, which simplifies to $\blueE{27\,\text{g}}$.

###Putting it together and solving

The new solution needs to have a $40\%$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}\\\\
\goldE{0.4}\cdot \redE{(90-m)} &= \blueE{27}\\\\
36-0.4m &=27\\\\
9 &=0.4m\\\\
22.5 &=m
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

A mass of $22.5\,\text{g}$ of water need to evaporate to reach the higher concentration.

options.definition: A mixture where something is evenly mixed throughout a liquid

assessment_items.question_content:
A factory has a batch of hand sanitizer with $50\%$ alcohol content.

**How much pure ($100\%$ concentration) alcohol should they include in a $600\,\text{mL}$ bottle to make a $68\%$ mixture?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{mL}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}}{\redE{\text{total volume}}}$

Since the total volume will be greater than $0\,\text{mL}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $v$ to represent the volume, in milliliters, of the pure alcohol and solve for $v$.

###Representing the total volume

The total volume in the bottle is $\redE{600\,\text{mL}}$.

###Representing the ingredient volume

The same relationship among concentration, total volume, and ingredient volume exists for the batch of hand sanitizer and the pure alcohol.

The batch of hand sanitizer has a $\goldE{50\%}$ concentration of alcohol. Since $v$ is the volume of pure alcohol, $\redE{600\,\text{mL}-v}$ is the volume of other ingredients from the batch.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.5} \cdot (\redE{600\,\text{mL}-v})&= \blueE{300\,\text{mL}-0.5v}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{(300-0.5v)\,\text{mL}}$ of alcohol in the hand sanitizer from the batch.

The pure alcohol has a $\goldE{100\%}$ concentration of alcohol.

$\goldE{1.00} \cdot \redE{v} = \blueE{v}$

There are $\blueE{v\,\text{mL}}$ of alcohol in the pure alcohol.

There will be $\blueE{(300-0.5v+v)\,\text{mL}}$ of alcohol in the mixture, which simplifies to $\blueE{(300+0.5v)\,\text{mL}}$.

###Putting it together and solving

The sanitizer in the bottle needs to have a $68\%$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.68}\cdot \redE{\redE{600\,\text{mL}}} &= \blueE{300\,\text{mL}+0.5v}\\\\
408\,\text{mL} &= \blueE{300\,\text{mL}+0.5v}\\\\
108\,\text{mL}&= 0.5v\\\\
216\,\text{mL}&=v
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

They should include $216\,\text{mL}$ of pure alcohol in the bottle.

assessment_items.question_content:
A seasoning blend is $4\%$ onion powder, by mass.

**How much pure onion powder should they include in a $72\,\text{g}$ bottle to make the final blend have $20\%$ onion powder?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{g}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}}{\redE{\text{total mass}}}$

Since the total mass will be greater than $0\,\text{g}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $m$ to represent the mass, in grams, of the added onion powder and solve for $m$.

###Representing the total mass

The total mass in the bottle is $\redE{72\,\text{g}}$.

###Representing the ingredient mass

The same relationship among concentration, total mass, and ingredient mass exists for the original seasoning blend and the added onion powder.

The seasoning blend has a $\goldE{4\%}$ concentration of onion powder. Since $m$ is the mass of pure onion powder, $\redE{72\,\text{g}-m}$ is the mass of the original seasoning blend.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}\\\\
\goldE{0.04} \cdot (\redE{72\,\text{g}-m})&= \blueE{2.88\,\text{g}-0.04m}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{2.88\,\text{g}-0.04m}$ of onion powder in the bottle from the original seasoning blend.

The pure onion powder has a $\goldE{100\%}$ concentration of onion powder.

$\goldE{1.00} \cdot \redE{m} = \blueE{m}$

There are $\blueE{m}$ of onion powder in the pure onion powder.

There will be $\blueE{2.88\,\text{g}-0.04m+m}$ of onion powder in the bottle, which simplifies to $\blueE{2.88\,\text{g}+0.96m}$.

###Putting it together and solving

The seasoning blend in the bottle needs to have a $20\%$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total mass}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient mass}}\\\\
\goldE{0.2}\cdot \redE{\redE{72\,\text{g}}} &= \blueE{2.88\,\text{g}+0.96m}\\\\
14.4\,\text{g} &= 2.88\,\text{g} + 0.96m\\\\
11.52 &= 0.96m\\\\
12 &= m
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

They should include $12\,\text{g}$ of pure onion powder in the bottle.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has $35\%$ and $3\%$ hydrogen peroxide [[☃ definition 1]]. They need an $8\,\text{L}$ container with a $12\%$ concentration of hydrogen peroxide.

**How much of the $3\%$ hydrogen peroxide solution should they include in the container?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{L}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{concentration}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}}{\redE{\text{total volume}}}$

Since the total volume will be greater than $0\,\text{L}$, we can rewrite the relationship as a linear equation.

$\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $v$ to represent the volume, in liters, of the $3\%$ hydrogen peroxide solution and solve for $v$.

###Representing the total volume

The total volume in the container is $\redE{8\,\text{L}}$.

###Representing the ingredient volume

The same relationship among concentration, total volume, and ingredient volume exists for each solution.

The stronger concentration has a $\goldE{35\%}$ concentration of hydrogen peroxide. Since $v$ is the volume of the weaker concentration, $\redE{8\,\text{L}-v}$ is the volume of the stronger concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}})&= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.35} \cdot (\redE{8\,\text{L}-v})&= \blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.35v}
\end{align}$

There are $\blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.35v}$ of hydrogen peroxide in the stronger concentration.

The weaker concentration has a $\goldE{3\%}$ concentration of hydrogen peroxide.

$\goldE{0.03} \cdot \redE{v} = \blueE{0.03v}$

There are $\blueE{0.03v\,\text{L}}$ of hydrogen peroxide in the weaker concentration.

There will be $\blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.35v+0.03v}$ of hydrogen peroxide in the container, which simplifies to $\blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.32v}$.

###Putting it together and solving

The solution in the container needs to have a $12\%$ concentration.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{concentration}}\cdot (\redE{\text{total volume}}) &= \blueE{\text{ingredient volume}}\\\\
\goldE{0.12}\cdot \redE{\redE{8\,\text{L}}} &= \blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.32v}\\\\
0.96\,\text{L} &= \blueE{2.8\,\text{L}-0.32v}\\\\
0.32v &= 1.84\,\text{L}\\\\
v &=5.75\,\text{L}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

They should include $5.75\,\text{L}$ of the $3\%$ solution in the container.

options.definition: Mixtures where something is evenly mixed throughout a liquid

assessment_items.question_content:
Deshawn and Tyriq can weed their garden in $30$ minutes together. Alone, it takes Tyriq $75$ minutes to weed the garden.

**How long does it take Deshawn to weed the garden alone?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Strategy

Because Deshawn and Tyriq work for the same amount of time, we can directly combine their work rates.

$(\goldE{\text{Deshawn's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{Tyriq's rate}})= \blueE{\text{total rate}}$

For each rate, we can use the following relationship:

${\text{work rate}} = \dfrac{{\text{total work}}}{{\text{time}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $d$ to represent the time, in minutes, it takes Deshawn to weed the garden alone and solve for $d$.

###Representing each rate

Tyriq's work rate is $\redE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{75\text{ min}}}$.

Deshawn's work rate is $\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}}$.

Their combined work rate is $\blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{30\text{ min}}}$.

###Solving for Deshawn's time

$\begin{align}
(\goldE{\text{Deshawn's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{Tyriq's rate}}) &= \blueE{\text{total rate}}\\\\
\left(\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}}\right) + \left(\redE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{75\text{ min}}}\right) &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{30\text{ min}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{30\text{ min}}} - \redE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{75\text{ min}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{5\text{ gardens}}{150\text{ min}}} - \redE{\dfrac{2\text{ gardens}}{150\text{ min}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}} &= \dfrac{3\text{ gardens}}{150\text{ min}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{d}} &= \dfrac{1\text{ garden}}{50\text{ min}}\\\\
d&= 50\text{ min}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

It takes Deshawn $50$ minutes to weed the garden alone.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dane is using two differently sized water pumps to clean up flooded water. The larger pump can remove the water alone in $240\,\text{min}$. The smaller pump can remove the water alone in $400\,\text{min}$.

**How long would it take the pumps to remove the water working together?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Strategy

Because the pumps work for the same amount of time, we can directly combine their work rates.

$(\goldE{\text{larger pump's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{smaller pump's rate}})= \blueE{\text{total rate}}$

For each rate, we can use the following relationship:

${\text{work rate}} = \dfrac{{\text{total work}}}{{\text{time}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $t$ to represent the time, in minutes, it takes the pumps when working together, then solve for $t$.

###Representing each rate

The larger pump's rate is $\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{240\text{ min}}}$.

The smaller pump's rate is $\redE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{400\,\text{m}}}$.

Their combined work rate is $\blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{t}}$.

###Solving for the combined work rate

$\begin{align}
(\goldE{\text{larger pump's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{smaller pump's rate}}) &= \blueE{\text{total rate}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{240\text{ min}}} + \redE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{400\,\text{m}}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{t}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{5\text{ jobs}}{1{,}200\text{ min}}} + \redE{\dfrac{3\text{ jobs}}{1{,}200\,\text{m}}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{t}}\\\\
{\dfrac{8\text{ jobs}}{1{,}200\text{ min}}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{t}}\\\\
{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{150\text{ min}}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ job}}{t}}\\\\
150\,\text{min}&=t
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

It takes the pumps $150$ minutes to remove the water working together.

assessment_items.question_content:
Together, Raquel and Miray can spread the mulch through the yard in $2$ hours. Raquel would take $0.8$ times as many hours as Miray would take to spread the mulch alone.

**How long would it take Miray to spread the mulch alone?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
###Strategy

Because Raquel and Miray work for the same amount of time, we can directly combine their work rates.

$(\goldE{\text{Raquel's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{Miray's rate}})= \blueE{\text{total rate}}$

For each rate, we can use the following relationship:

${\text{work rate}} = \dfrac{{\text{total work}}}{{\text{time}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. We can use $m$ to represent the time, in hours, it takes Miray to spread the mulch alone and solve for $m$.

###Representing each rate

Miray's work rate is $\redE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{m}}$.

It would take Raquel $0.8m$ hours to spread the mulch. Raquel's work rate is $\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{0.8m}}$.

Their combined work rate is $\blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{2\text{ h}}}$.

###Solving for Miray's time

$\begin{align}
(\goldE{\text{Raquel's rate}}) + (\redE{\text{Miray's rate}}) &= \blueE{\text{total rate}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{0.8m}} + \redE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{m}} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{2\text{ h}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{0.8\cdot \cancel{m}}} \cdot \cancel{m} + \redE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{1\cdot \cancel{m}}} \cdot \cancel{m} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{2\text{ h}}} \cdot {m}\\\\
1.25\,\text{yard} + 1\,\text{yard} &= \blueE{\dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{2\text{ h}}} \cdot {m}\\\\
\dfrac{2\text{ h}}{1\cancel{\text{ yard}}} \cdot 2.25\,\cancel{\text{yard}} &= \dfrac{\cancel{2\text{ h}}}{\cancel{1\text{ yard}}} \cdot \blueE{\dfrac{\cancel{1\text{ yard}}}{\cancel{2\text{ h}}}} \cdot {m}\\\\
4.5\,\text{h} &= m
\end{align}$

*Note: Multiplying both sides by $m$ is valid because $m\neq0$.*

###Solution

It would take Miray $4.5$ hours to spread the mulch alone.

assessment_items.question_content:
Naira paddled $4.5\,\text{km}$ with the current in the same amount of time as it took her to paddle $3\,\text{km}$ against the current. Naira paddled at an average rate of $5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$ relative to the water each way. Assume the speed of the current was constant.

**What was the speed of the current?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\goldE{\text{time with current}} = \redE{\text{time against current}}$

For each distance, we can use the following relationship:

$\blueE{\text{distance}} = \maroonE{\text{rate}} \cdot \purpleD{{\text{time}}}$

Since the rate does not equal $0\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$ in either direction, we can rewrite the relationship as a rational equation.

$\purpleD{\text{time}} = \dfrac{\blueE{\text{distance}}}{\maroonE{\text{rate}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. Let's use $c$ to represent the speed of the current, in kilometers per hour, then solve for $c$.

###Representing each time

Naira's paddling rate *with* the current is $5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c$.  So Naira's time with the current is $\goldE{\dfrac{4.5\,\text{km}}{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c\right)}}$.

Naira's paddling rate *against* the current is $5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c$.  So Naira's time against the current is $\redE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c\right)}}$.

###Solving for the speed of the current

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{time with current}} &= \redE{\text{time against current}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{4.5\,\text{km}}{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c\right)}} &= \redE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c\right)}} \\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{4.5\,\text{km}}{\cancel{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c\right)}}} \cdot \cancel{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c\right)} \cdot \left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c\right) &= \redE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{\cancel{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c\right)}}} \cdot \left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}+c\right) \cdot \cancel{\left(5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}-c\right)}  \\\\
22.5\,\dfrac{\text{km}^2}{\text{h}}-4.5c\,\text{km} &= 15\,\dfrac{\text{km}^2}{\text{h}}+3c\,\text{km} \\\\
7.5\,\dfrac{\text{km}\cdot\text{km}}{\text{h}}&=7.5c\,\text{km} \\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{7.5}\,\dfrac{\text{km}\cdot\cancel{\text{km}}}{\text{h}}}{\cancel{7.5}\,\cancel{\text{km}}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{7.5}c\,\cancel{\text{km}}}{\cancel{7.5}\,\cancel{\text{km}}}\\\\
1\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} &=c
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Solution

The speed of the current was $1\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$.

answers.message: Naira paddles at $5\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$ *relative to the water*, not once we include the current.

assessment_items.question_content:
Malik walked up and back down an empty $18\,\text{m}$ long [[☃ definition 1]] at a constant rate. Since the escalator was going up at the time, it took him only $22.5\,\text{s}$. Going down took Malik $90\,\text{s}$. Assume the escalator speed is constant.

**What was Malik's walking speed?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$
hints:
###Strategy

$(\goldE{\text{distance up}}) = (\redE{\text{distance down}})$

For each distance, we can use the following relationship:

${\text{distance}} = {\text{rate}} \cdot {{\text{time}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. Let's use $m$ to represent Malik's walking speed, in meters per second. We can use $r$ to represent the speed of the escalator, in meters per second.

We can solve for one of our unknowns. If that value is $m$, then we'll know Malik's speed. Otherwise, we can substitute the value we find back into our model solve for $m$.

###Representing each distance

Malik's total rate up the escalator is $m+r$.  So Malik's distance up the escalator is $\goldE{\left(m+r\right)22.5\,\text{s}}$.

Malik's total rate down the escalator is $m-r$.  So Malik's distance down the escalator is $\redE{\left(m-r\right)90\,\text{s}}$.

###Solving for a rate

$\begin{align}
(\goldE{\text{distance up}}) &= (\redE{\text{distance down}})\\\\
\goldE{\left(m+r\right)22.5\,\text{s}} &= \redE{\left(m-r\right)90\,\text{s}}\\\\
22.5m\,\text{s}+22.5r\,\text{s}&=90m\,\text{s}-90r\,\text{s}\\\\
112.5r\,\text{s}&=67.5m\,\text{s}\\\\
r&=\dfrac{67.5m\,\cancel{\text{s}}}{112.5\,\cancel{\text{s}}}\\\\
r&=0.6m
\end{align}$

The escalator's speed is $60\%$ of Malik's speed.

###Solving for Malik's speed

Let's consider just when Malik was going down the escalator.

$\begin{align}
{\text{distance}} &= {\text{rate}} \cdot {{\text{time}}}\\\\
18\,\text{m} &= \redE{\left(m-r\right)90\,\text{s}}\\\\
18\,\text{m} &= \redE{\left(m-0.6m\right)90\,\text{s}}\\\\
18\,\text{m} &= \redE{\left(0.4m\right)90\,\text{s}}\\\\
\dfrac{18\,\text{m}}{(0.4)90\,\text{s}} &= \dfrac{\left(\cancel{0.4}m\right)\cancel{90\,\text{s}}}{\left(\cancel{0.4}\right)\cancel{90\,\text{s}}}\\\\
\purpleD{0.5\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}&=\purpleD{m}
\end{align}$

###Solution

Malik walked at a rate of $\purpleD{0.5\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}$.

options.definition: A moving staircase that lifts or lowers people between floors

answers.message: This is the combined speed when Malik is walking up the escalator and the escalator is going up.
This is the combined speed when Malik is walking down the escalator and the escalator is going up.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nizhoni adds water at a constant rate to a $2\,\text{L}$ [[☃ definition 1]] in a water filtering pitcher until it is full. At the same time, water goes through the filter and out of the reservoir, into the pitcher. It takes $\dfrac{5}{11}$ minutes to fill the reservoir, then another $3\dfrac{1}{3}$ minutes for the reservoir to empty.

**At what rate does Nizhoni add water to the reservoir?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}$
hints:
###Strategy

$\blueE{\text{final volume}} = \purpleD{\text{(starting volume)}}+\redE{\text{(change in volume)}}$

For each change in volume, we can use the following relationship:

${\text{change in volume}} = {\text{rate}} \cdot {{\text{time}}}$

Let's fill in the information we know. Let's use $n$ to represent the rate, in liters per minute, that Nizhoni adds water to the reservoir. We can use $f$ to represent the rate, in liters per minute, that the filter empties water from the reservoir.

We can solve for one of our unknowns. If that value is $n$, then we'll know the rate Nizhoni adds the water. Otherwise, we can substitute the value we find back into our model solve for $n$.

###Representing each change in volume

The rate of change in the reservoir when Nizhoni is filling and the filter is emptying is $n-f$.  So the change in volume is $\goldE{\left(n-f\right)\dfrac{5}{11}\,\text{min}}$.

The rate of change in the reservoir when only the filter is emptying is $-f$.  So the change in volume is $\redE{\left(-f\right)\dfrac{10}{3}\,\text{min}}$.

###Solving for the filter rate

Let's model the reservoir emptying first, since there are fewer unknowns. When the reservoir is emptying, it goes from $2\,\text{L}$ to $0\,\text{L}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{\text{final volume}} &= \purpleD{\text{(starting volume)}}+\redE{\text{(change in volume)}}\\\\
\blueE{0\,\text{L}} &= \purpleD{2\,\text{L}}+\redE{\left(-f\right)\dfrac{10}{3}\,\text{min}}\\\\
f\cdot\dfrac{10\,\text{min}}{3}&=2\,\text{L}\\\\
f\cdot\dfrac{10\,\text{min}}{3}\cdot\dfrac{3}{10\,\text{min}}&=2\,\text{L}\cdot\dfrac{3}{10\,\text{min}}\\\\
f&=\dfrac{3}{5}\,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}
\end{align}$

The filter empties water from the reservoir at a rate of $\dfrac{3}{5}\,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}$.

###Solving for Nizhoni's rate

Now we can solve for the rate that Nizhoni adds water to the reservoir. When the reservoir is filling, it goes from $0\,\text{L}$ to $2\,\text{L}$.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{\text{final volume}} &= \purpleD{\text{(starting volume)}}+\goldE{\text{(change in volume)}}\\\\
\blueE{2\,\text{L}} &= \purpleD{0\,\text{L}}+\goldE{\left(n-f\right)\dfrac{5}{11}\,\text{min}}\\\\
\blueE{2\,\text{L}} &= \purpleD{0\,\text{L}}+\goldE{\left(n-\dfrac{3}{5}\,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}\right)\dfrac{5}{11}\,\text{min}}\\\\
\blueE{2\,\text{L}} &=\goldE{n\cdot \dfrac{5}{11}\,\text{min}-\dfrac{3}{11}\,\text{L}}\\\\
\dfrac{25}{11}\,\text{L}&= n\cdot \dfrac{5}{11}\,\text{min}\\\\
\dfrac{25}{\cancel{11}}\,\text{L}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{11}}{5\,\text{min}}&= n\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{5}}{\cancel{11}}\,\cancel{\text{min}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{11}}{\cancel{5}\,\cancel{\text{min}}}\\\\
5\,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}&=n
\end{align}$

###Solution

Nizhoni adds water to the reservoir at a rate of $5\,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}$.
